title: Orangutans Seek Out Medicinal Plants
author: Devin Reese
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementZoologyOrangutans Seek Out Medicinal PlantsIn fact, they’re some of the same plants used pharmacologically by local Indigenous peopleDRBy Devin Reese2:00 PM CDT on June 25, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyIn 2024, biologists reported that a wild Sumatran orangutan named Raku treated his own facial wound with a poultice of chewed-up leaves. They weren’t just any leaves either—Raku selected leaves from a plant vine, Fibraurea tinctoria, known to have medicinal properties, from analgesic pain relief to antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects. Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 1049.7K 1Former ‘World’s Ugliest Animal’ Now Crowned ‘Fish of the Year’ShareNextStayNow, a recent study in Scientific Reports has found that Raku’s self-medicating moment wasn’t a one-off event, but rather part of a broader suite of orangutan behaviors. Researchers from the United Kingdom and Indonesia collected behavioral data for more than 20 years on a population of about 100 wild Bornean orangutans living in a peat-swamp forest. Observations of feeding included the species of plant as well as the parts eaten, whether fruit, seed, skin, pulp, pith, or bark. Two coauthors local to the study site offered ethnobotanical knowledge about medicinal properties and uses of the plants by humans.A total of 12,236 feeding events were recorded, yielding 202 distinct plant species. Of those, about 30 percent were recognized as medicinal plants by the Indigenous Dayak communities of the region. Furthermore, orangutan consumption of the medicinal plants was found to be non-random.Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisement
